title: The meter is running
summary: Anthropic's repeatedly-extended free window for Claude Fable 5 finally closed at 11:59:59 PM Pacific on July 19, moving Pro and Team Standard to metered per-token pricing while Moonshot, facing the same scarcity from the other side, simply stopped taking new Kimi K3 subscriptions. Then a terminal-agent benchmark that grades partial progress instead of pass/fail finds the best of fifteen frontier models finishing only 10.9% of long tasks — at about 85 minutes and 9.9 million tokens each — and a paper that reorganizes an agent harness codebase around behaviors rather than modules.

sources.title: Anthropic — Claude Fable 5 on your plan (support)
Moonshot AI pauses new Kimi K3 subscriptions on demand surge
Long-Horizon-Terminal-Bench: dense-reward grading for hours-long terminal agent tasks
Harness Handbook: making evolving agent harnesses navigable and editable
Ring-Zero: scaling zero-RL reasoning to 1 trillion parameters
Xinhua — 29 countries sign agreement creating WAICO
AI advice study: users less accurate, more confident (The Next Web)
Simon Willison — Sam Altman's 2022 email on releasing a local GPT-3-class model

topics: Claude Fable 5 free inclusion ends: the promotion allowing up to 50% of weekly subscription limits on Fable 5 at no extra cost ended July 19 2026 at 11:59:59 PM PT, after slipping from June 22 to July 7 to July 12 to July 19; Max and Team Premium keep Fable 5 as a standard part of the plan at 50% of weekly limits, Pro and Team Standard move to credits with a one-time credit (amount not stated on Anthropic's own pages, so not stated on air) then metered pricing of $10 per million input tokens and $50 per million output tokens; r/Anthropic complaints centered on mid-session interruptions and lost subagent work. Paired on air with Moonshot pausing new consumer Kimi K3 subscriptions after requests sharply exceeded forecasts and approached the limits of existing clusters — two responses to the same compute scarcity. Ada's framing: metering is the older idea (billing by the processor-second), flat rate arrived with abundance, so re-metering is a scarcity signal.

Long-Horizon-Terminal-Bench: 46 long-horizon terminal tasks across nine categories, graded by a dense reward scheme that decomposes each task into graded subtasks for partial credit instead of binary pass/fail; across 15 frontier models the best (GPT-5.5) reaches 15.2% pass@1 at a 0.95 partial-reward threshold and 10.9% at 1.0 — the gap meaning a real slice of runs die in the final 5% of the job; average task consumes 85.3 minutes wall-clock and ~9.9M tokens. Authors note the dense scheme 'reveals that many runs achieve partial progress' previously scored as zero. On-air pushback from Alan: partial credit flatters a system, since 95% of a database migration is worse than zero in production, and at ~$100/task floor with ~11% deployable success that's roughly nine attempts per completed task. Caveat stated: single benchmark, authors' own runs.

Harness Handbook: automatically reorganizes an agent-harness codebase around runtime behaviors rather than modules, synthesized from source via static analysis plus LLM-assisted structuring; on two open-source harnesses the authors report handbook guidance increased win rates by 10-19 percentage points while cutting planning tokens by 8-13%. On-air caveat: the static-analysis half is checkable, the LLM-structuring half is documentation generation, whose classic failure is being confidently wrong — use it as a search tool over candidate locations, not a source of truth.

threads.name: Claude Fable 5 metering rollout
threads.status: The free-inclusion promotion ended July 19 2026 at 11:59:59 PM PT after four extensions (June 22, July 7, July 12, July 19). Max and Team Premium retain 50% of weekly limits as a standard plan feature; Pro and Team Standard move to a one-time credit then metered pricing at $10/$50 per million input/output tokens.
threads.arc: A free window that kept being extended rather than honored or cancelled has now closed, converting subscription-plan access to the frontier model into per-token billing — and arriving the same week a rival lab stopped selling subscriptions outright for capacity reasons.

repeat_coverage.repeat_of: Fable 5 access deadline, an active moving thread across recent episodes (2026-07-17/18)
repeat_coverage.new_development: The deadline actually landed — the promotion ended at 11:59:59 PM PT on July 19 and Pro/Team Standard are now on metered per-token pricing at $10 and $50 per million tokens, which is the first time the plan split and the concrete rates apply rather than being a pending date.

repeat_coverage.story: Moonshot pauses new Kimi K3 subscriptions (beat inside the Fable 5 dive)
repeat_coverage.new_development: Moonshot stopped taking new consumer subscriptions entirely, citing requests sharply exceeding forecasts and approaching the limits of existing clusters — a capacity event, not the benchmark-position story previously covered.

dives.story: Claude Fable 5 free inclusion ends; Pro and Team Standard go metered
dives.why_it_matters: Anyone running agentic work on a Claude subscription just had their cost model change from a flat ceiling to per-token billing at $10/$50 per million, which specifically punishes long agent runs that die mid-session — you pay for every token of work you never got the result of.

dives.story: Long-Horizon-Terminal-Bench: dense-reward grading for hours-long agent tasks
dives.why_it_matters: Anyone evaluating whether to deploy an autonomous agent on multi-hour work gets both a number to plan against — 10.9% completion at ~$100 and ~85 minutes a task, roughly nine attempts per success — and a grading method that shows where the failures cluster, which binary pass/fail benchmarks were scoring as an undifferentiated zero.

dives.story: Harness Handbook: mapping an agent harness by behavior instead of by module
dives.why_it_matters: Anyone maintaining a production agent harness knows bug reports arrive organized by behavior while the code is organized by module; this is a concrete, measured attempt to close that gap, reporting better outcomes and fewer planning tokens at once.

concepts_taught: dense-reward / partial-credit grading for long-horizon agent benchmarks: why binary pass-fail destroys information on multi-hour tasks, how decomposing a task into graded subtasks yields a 0-to-1 score, and how the gap between the 0.95 and 1.0 thresholds localizes where runs die
why per-token pricing runs roughly five to one output-over-input: prompts are read in one parallel pass while output is generated sequentially, one token at a time
